title: RESTORE Act
summary: The RESTORE Act (Resources and Ecosystems Sustainability, Tourist Opportunities, and Revived Economies of the Gulf Coast States Act) was signed into law on July 6, 2012, establishing the Gulf Coast Restoration Trust Fund to manage penalties from the Deepwater Horizon oil spill. The Act directs 80% of administrative and civil penalties paid by responsible parties under the Federal Water Pollution Control Act into the Trust Fund, with distributions allocated across five components: Direct Component (35%), Comprehensive Plan Component (30%), Spill Impact Component (30%), and Science/Research Programs (5%).
key_points: RESTORE Act signed July 6, 2012, established Gulf Coast Restoration Trust Fund in U.S. Treasury
Eighty percent of Deepwater Horizon penalties deposited into Trust Fund for Gulf Coast restoration
Trust Fund distribution: 35% Direct Component (administered by Treasury to Gulf States), 30% Comprehensive Plan Component (administered by Gulf Coast Ecosystem Restoration Council)
Spill Impact Component (30%) allocated to Gulf Coast States via state expenditure plans approved by Council
Five percent plus interest split between NOAA Science Program and Centers of Excellence Research Grants
Treasury final rule at 31 C.F.R. Part 34 effective February 12, 2016
why_it_matters: This source is informational material about a historical environmental trust fund established over a decade ago. It does not contain current macro catalysts, policy signals, or market-moving information. The content describes the administrative structure and allocation formulas for Gulf Coast restoration funding derived from Deepwater Horizon oil spill penalties. It is not relevant for real-time financial decision-making or macro market analysis.
